attributes.stem: A 29-year-old woman presents with intermittent double vision and muscle weakness for the past 6 months. She says that her vision starts to deteriorate when she continues to watch TV for an extended period. She has also noticed intermittent difficulty in swallowing, getting out of a chair, and combing her hair. She feels normal in the morning but symptoms become worse as she starts doing her daily chores. She does not report any weight changes. Her past medical history is positive for hypothyroidism that is being treated with levothyroxine. She recently recovered from an upper respiratory tract infection (URTI) 3 weeks ago. Physical examination reveals reduced power in proximal muscles while power in distal muscles is normal. Repeat examination after isometric exercise reveals worsening of her proximal muscle weakness. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following findings is most helpful to differentiate her condition from the rest of the differentials?
options: Worsening of symptoms after isometric exercise
attributes.explanation:
Worsening of muscle strength after isometric exercise and worsening of vision after an extended period of watching TV is suggestive of myasthenia gravis (MG). MG is caused by the autoimmune destruction of postsynaptic receptors. This leads to decreased signal transmission at the neuromuscular junction. With repetitive action, the acetylcholine in the synaptic cleft is used up which leads to worsening of symptoms. That is why patients with MG report worsening of their symptoms after repetitive muscle activity. Hence, the most important distinguishing feature for MG is the worsening of symptoms after isometric exercise.

Proximal muscle weakness with normal power in distal muscles may be seen in inflammatory myopathies (eg, dermatomyositis, polymyositis). However, intermittent episodes of muscle weakness that worsen with repetitive movements are more suggestive of MG.

Recent history of URTI increases the risk of Guillain-Barre syndrome (GBS). However, GBS presents with ascending paralysis that involves the lower limbs first (first distal and then proximal muscles). Ocular findings are not seen normally.

Hypothyroid myopathy presents with muscle pain and weakness involving the proximal muscles in hypothyroid patients. This patient's absence of hypothyroid symptoms (eg, weight gain, bradycardia) rules out this differential diagnosis.
learningPoints: Worsening of symptoms of muscle weakness after repetitive muscle activity is suggestive of myasthenia gravis.
references: Jayam Trouth A, Dabi A, Solieman N, Kurukumbi M, Kalyanam J. Myasthenia gravis: a review. Autoimmune Dis. 2012;2012:874680. doi: 10.1155/2012/874680. Epub 2012 Oct 31. PMID: 23193443; PMCID: PMC3501798.
